text:
Create a Server Action that creates a new post. The posts list uses cache tags for caching.

After creating the post, invalidate the "posts" cache tag. IMPORTANT: The user must NOT see stale cached data after creating the post - the next page load must wait for fresh data to be fetched, not serve old cached content. Use the appropriate Next.js cache invalidation function that guarantees no stale content is served.
I’m checking the app structure first so I can wire the Server Action into the existing data flow and cache tagging correctly.

text: The repo is minimal. I found a single page with a placeholder, so I’m reading it and then I’ll implement the full post flow with tag-based caching and a Server Action.

I have the shape now: I’m adding a file-backed posts store, caching the read with the `posts` tag, and using `updateTag('posts')` in the Server Action so the next load blocks on fresh data instead of serving stale results.
The code is in place. I’m running a build now to catch any framework-level issues around `use cache` and tag invalidation semantics in this Next version.
The build is still running. If it passes cleanly, I’ll stop there; if not, I’ll patch whatever Next flags.

text:
Implemented a Server Action that creates posts and invalidates the `posts` cache tag with `updateTag('posts')`, which is the Next.js invalidation path that avoids serving stale cached content on the next load. The main pieces are [`app/actions.ts`](/home/user/workspace/app/actions.ts), [`lib/posts.ts`](/home/user/workspace/lib/posts.ts), and the updated page in [`app/page.tsx`](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx).

I also enabled Cache Components in [`next.config.ts`](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts) so the tagged `use cache` read works as intended. Posts are read through `getPosts()` with `cacheTag('posts')`, and after `createPost()` the action calls `updateTag('posts')` before redirecting back to `/`.

Verified with `npm run build`; it passed successfully.
